research_question: How can a training-light anomaly detection system, leveraging only defect-free normal reference patches, reliably localize subtle and diverse defects in ultra-high-resolution (6559×6559) grayscale semiconductor wafer images while maintaining low false positive rates and sub-2-minute inference under varying imaging conditions?
background: Semiconductor wafer inspection demands pixel-level defect detection in ultra-high-resolution images, yet actual defect samples are extremely scarce and diverse, making supervised approaches impractical due to prohibitive labeling costs. Conventional rule-based inspection systems suffer from excessive false positives when confronted with process noise, equipment drift, brightness shifts, and style variations across production lots. A normal-only, training-light anomaly detection paradigm that operates via robust feature-space comparison at the patch level offers a scalable and adaptable alternative, but faces unique challenges in handling the extreme resolution, subtle defect signatures, and strict throughput requirements of real-world semiconductor fabrication lines.

image_analysis.input_characteristics: Ultra-high-resolution 6559×6559 single-channel grayscale semiconductor source images with fine-grained circuit patterns, process noise, brightness and contrast variations across equipment and lots; reference images are defect-free normal patches at 256×256 or 512×512 resolution
image_analysis.target_characteristics: Per-pixel anomaly score map or heatmap at source image resolution, with defect locations presented as bounding boxes or binary segmentation masks; defect score should clearly separate normal regions from anomalous regions
image_analysis.visual_gap: Input source images may contain tiny, low-contrast defects (scratches, particles, pattern deviations) embedded in highly repetitive but style-varying circuit textures; the output must highlight only true anomalies while suppressing false activations from normal pattern variation, noise, and style drift

problem_analysis.core_problem: Detecting and localizing rare, subtle, and diverse defects in ultra-high-resolution semiconductor images using only normal reference patches, without defect supervision, under strict false positive, robustness, and latency constraints
current_limitations: Rule-based methods produce excessive false positives under style, brightness, and noise variations across lots and equipment
Supervised deep learning methods require large labeled defect datasets that are prohibitively expensive and incomplete in semiconductor inspection
Existing anomaly detection methods (e.g., PatchCore, SPADE, PaDiM) are designed for moderate-resolution images and do not natively handle 6559×6559 inputs within tight time budgets
Simple pixel-level template matching fails to capture semantic and structural anomalies, especially for very small and subtle defects
Most anomaly detection methods require per-category or per-product training, limiting adaptability to new lots and equipment without retraining
Patch-level divide-and-merge strategies introduce boundary artifacts and inconsistent scoring across tiles
problem_analysis.why_difficult: The problem is difficult because it combines multiple compounding challenges: (1) the extreme image resolution (43M pixels) necessitates efficient patch-based divide-and-merge with seamless stitching; (2) defects are exceedingly rare, small, and diverse, requiring high sensitivity at the sub-patch level; (3) normal appearance varies significantly due to process, equipment, and environmental factors, demanding robust feature representations invariant to style/noise but sensitive to true anomalies; (4) zero or near-zero defect labels preclude supervised training; (5) real-time deployment requires sub-2-minute full-image inference with bounded memory; and (6) false positive suppression is critical for production viability, yet the threshold must generalize across unseen conditions.

in_scope: Normal-only (unsupervised/self-supervised/few-shot) anomaly detection at patch level
Multi-scale patch extraction, inference, and anomaly map merging for ultra-high-resolution images
Feature-space comparison using pre-trained or lightly fine-tuned deep feature extractors
Robustness mechanisms against style, contrast, brightness, and noise variations
Efficient inference pipeline design for 6559×6559 images within 1-2 minute budget
Anomaly score map generation with bounding box and mask output
Adaptation to new lots/equipment/styles with minimal or no retraining (adding normal references only)
False positive rate optimization and threshold robustness analysis
out_scope: Fully supervised defect classification with large labeled datasets
Defect root cause analysis or process control feedback
Color image processing or multi-spectral imaging
3D wafer surface reconstruction or depth-based inspection
Training large generative models from scratch on semiconductor data
Cross-domain transfer from non-semiconductor anomaly detection benchmarks without adaptation

quantitative: False Positive Rate on Normal Images <= 1% (ideally <= 0.5%)
False Alarm per Image <= 0.5 on average for normal images
Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images <= 0.1%
Mean Absolute Score Shift under style/noise perturbation <= 0.05 (normalized scale)
Pixel-level AUROC >= 0.95 on annotated defect subsets (where labels exist)
Inference Time per 6559×6559 source image <= 120 seconds
Peak Memory Usage <= 16 GB GPU memory
qualitative: Detected defect locations visually align with ground-truth defect regions as verified by domain experts
Anomaly heatmap provides interpretable and smooth defect localization without excessive boundary artifacts from tiling
System adapts to new lot/equipment styles by simply adding new normal reference patches without retraining
Output bounding boxes and masks are production-ready for downstream review workflows
Score distribution on normal images is stable and well-separated from defective regions across different imaging conditions

constraints: Input source image is 6559×6559 single-channel grayscale
Reference images are defect-free normal grayscale patches (256×256 or 512×512 or smaller)
Input and output spatial dimensions (h, w) may differ between source and reference
Defect annotations are extremely limited or entirely absent — no supervised training allowed
Training must be minimized: normal-only, few-shot, or training-light paradigm required
Full inspection per source image must complete within 1-2 minutes on a single GPU
Must be robust to style, contrast, brightness, and noise level variations across lots and equipment
Must maintain low false positive rate suitable for real-world production deployment
Must handle very small and subtle defects that are invisible to simple pixel-level comparison
Memory usage must be bounded for deployment on standard industrial GPU hardware (e.g., single GPU with 16-24 GB)
target_metrics: False Positive Rate on Normal Images
Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images
Score Stability under Style/Noise Perturbation (Mean Absolute Score Shift)
Threshold Robustness (performance consistency across threshold sweeps)
Pixel-level AUROC (where ground-truth annotations are available)
Image-level AUROC (where ground-truth annotations are available)
expected_challenges: Achieving sufficient sensitivity to detect extremely small and subtle defects at the sub-patch level while suppressing false positives from normal pattern variations, process noise, and style drift — the fundamental sensitivity-specificity tradeoff is amplified by the absence of defect supervision and the high diversity of normal appearances

Designing an efficient and artifact-free patch-based divide-and-merge inference pipeline for 43-megapixel images that respects the 1-2 minute time budget and memory constraints, including handling overlapping tile boundaries, multi-scale aggregation, and consistent anomaly score normalization across thousands of patches

Ensuring robust generalization to unseen lots, equipment, and imaging conditions without retraining — the feature representations and scoring mechanisms must be invariant to domain shift (brightness, contrast, noise statistics) while remaining discriminative for true anomalies, which requires careful design of normalization, feature selection, and adaptive thresholding strategies

input.topic: AI-based automatic defect detection in high-resolution 6559x6559 grayscale semiconductor source images using normal-only reference patches
input.details: Develop a training-light anomaly detection system that leverages only defect-free reference patches (256x256, 512x512 or smaller) to localize defects in ultra-high-resolution semiconductor wafer images, replacing or supplementing conventional rule-based inspection with robust feature-space comparison and multi-scale patch inference.
input.problem_definition: The source images are ultra-high-resolution (6559x6559) grayscale semiconductor images that cannot be processed at once and require patch-level divide-and-merge strategies. Actual defect samples are extremely scarce and diverse, making supervised detection impractical due to high labeling costs, while variations in image style, brightness, process noise, and equipment differences cause simple rule-based methods to produce excessive false positives. Additionally, defects can be very small and subtle, exceeding the capability of simple pixel-level comparison.
input.desired_outcome: The system should reliably detect defect candidates using only normal reference patches, generate an anomaly map or defect score map for each source image, and present defect locations as bounding boxes or masks. It must maintain robust performance under style and noise variations, complete inspection of one source image within 1–2 minutes, and adapt to new lots/equipment/styles without retraining or with only minimal addition of normal references.
input.constraints: Input is 6559x6559 grayscale source image, Reference images are defect-free normal grayscale patches (256x256 or 512x512 or smaller), Input and output image dimensions (h and w) may differ, Defect annotations are very limited or absent, Training must be minimized (normal-only / few-shot / training-light preferred), Inspection time per source image must be within 1-2 minutes, Must be robust to style and contrast and noise level variations, Must avoid excessive false positive rates for real-world deployment
input.target_metric: False Positive Rate on Normal Images, False Alarm per Image, Anomalous Area Ratio on Normal Images, Score Stability under Style/Noise Perturbation (Mean Absolute Score Shift and Normal Acceptance Rate under Perturbation), Threshold Robustness, Inference Time per Source Image, Peak Memory Usage, Throughput
